# Misheyakir

[Back to all zmanim](index.md)

## Misheyakir 10 Point 2 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun being 10.2° below geometric zenith.

Calculated when the sun is 10.2° below geometric zenith, which is based on the sun’s position 45 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir10Point2Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir10Point2Degrees`

## Misheyakir 11 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun being 11° below geometric zenith.

Calculated when the sun is 11° below geometric zenith, which is based on the sun’s position 48 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Degrees`

## Misheyakir 11 Point 5 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun being 11.5° below geometric zenith.

Calculated when the sun is 11.5° below geometric zenith, which is based on the sun’s position 52 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

The source notes that this calculation should be recalculated.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Point5Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Point5Degrees`

## Misheyakir 12 Point 85 Degrees

This is a very early misheyakir time that should only be used bish’as hadchak. Lechatchila, a later zman should be used.

A very early misheyakir time mentioned for use bish’as hadchak in the Birur Halacha [Tinyana](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50535&st=&pgnum=88) and [Tlisa'ah](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50537&st=&pgnum=31) in Orach Chaim siman 18 as 12.85°.

Calculated when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith. It is based on the sun’s position slightly later than 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

Actual calculations show the 57-minute time to be slightly more than 12.9°, while the Birur Halacha indicates that 12.85° is a slight chumra on a bedieved time, a difference of about 14 seconds at the equinox/equilux in Jerusalem.

A 12.9° time is also mentioned in Piskei Tshuvos siman 18, page 190, where a typo says it is equivalent to 60 minutes before sunrise, though at that point the sun is about 13.5° below the horizon.

The 57-minute based time is mentioned by the Minchas Yitzchak [vol. 9, siman 9](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=1601&st=&pgnum=21) as 15 minutes before alos hashachar, though he is not clear what location he refers to and does not mention a degree-based conversion.

The Kaf Hachaim [vol.1 siman 18, no. 18](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=8140&st=&pgnum=81) states that in Yerushalayim 60 fixed minutes are used year round. Calculations show that 60 fixed minutes in Yerushalayim ranges from 13.5° at the spring equinox to 11.5° at the summer solstice, while 57-minute misheyakir ranges from 12.9° at the winter equinox to 11° at the summer solstice.

At Vilnius’s latitude, around the summer solstice the sun will not reach this far below the horizon. On dates when both 12.85° and 12.9° can be calculated, the difference can exceed two minutes for about a week, while from December through March the difference is about 22 seconds.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees`

## Misheyakir 7 Point 65 Degrees

Misheyakir based on the sun being 7.65° below geometric zenith. This time is associated with a 35/36-minute misheyakir around the equinox / equilux, and with opinions and practices cited from Rabbi Moshe Feinstein, Rabbi Yisroel Taplin, Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky, the Satmar Rov, Lakewood, and Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky.

Calculated when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith. The degree value is based on a 35/36-minute zman [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/), when neshef is shortest.

[Rabbi Moshe Feinstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moshe_Feinstein) writes in [Ohr Hachaim Vol. 4, Ch. 6](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=14677&pgnum=7) that misheyakir in New York is 35–40 minutes before sunrise, a bit less than 8°.

[Rabbi Yisroel Taplin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yisroel_Taplin) in [Zmanei Yisrael](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/889556744) notes that [Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaakov_Kamenetsky) stated that it is not less than 36 minutes before sunrise, and maybe 40 minutes.

Sefer Yisrael Vehazmanim quotes the Tamar Yifrach in the name of the [Satmar Rov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Teitelbaum) that one should be stringent not to consider misheyakir before 36 minutes.

This is also the accepted [minhag](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minhag) in [Lakewood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lakewood_Township,_New_Jersey) used in the [Yeshiva](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beth_Medrash_Govoha).

Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky provided the time of 35/36 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time. Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based calculations to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees`

## Misheyakir 9 Point 5 Degrees

Misheyakir based on the sun being 9.5° below geometric zenith. It is associated with Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s 45-minute calculation in Baltimore and other cited uses of a 45-minute misheyakir.

Calculated when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith. This is based on [Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dovid_Kronglas) calculation of 45 minutes in Baltimore, as mentioned in [Divrei Chachamim No. 24](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=20287&pgnum=29) and brought by the [Birur Halacha, Tinyana, Ch. 18](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50535&pgnum=87).

[Rabbi Yaakov Yitzchok Neiman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacob_Isaac_Neiman) is cited in Kovetz Eitz Chaim Vol. 9, p. 202 as saying that the Vya’an Yosef did not want to rely on times earlier than 45 minutes in New York.

This zman is also used in calendars published by Rabbi Hershel Edelstein, and in calendars published by the [Edot Hamizrach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mizrahi_Jews) communities.

This also follows the opinion of [Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shmuel_Kamenetsky), who provided times of 36 and 45 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time. Since this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

This time may not be available in some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, where the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees`
